text: The surgeon uses a lung grasper through a five-millimeter port to retract the lung forward, releasing the pleura posteriorly. Station seven lymph node is removed anterior to the vagus nerve, revealing the inferior pulmonary ligament and vein. The left main bronchus, lower lobe bronchus, upper lobe bronchus, and secondary carina are exposed. The superior pulmonary vein is identified, noting the lingular vein's lower position. The inferior pulmonary vein is transected, followed by the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a TAMBODY-Five stapler to transect the basal pulmonary artery branch, ensuring preservation of the lingular branch without stenosis. The anterior fissure is taken after the artery to avoid compromising the lingular vein. The bronchus is cleaned and incised at the crux between the lower and upper lobes, with the incision extending through the secondary carina. The left main bronchus is visible in the distance, illuminated by the bronchoscope. The surgeon completes the resection with a V-shaped incision in the lower lobe bronchus, joining the cuts horizontally and severing the membranous part. The pulmonary artery, aorta, and superior vein are visible, with the lingular vein positioned slightly lower than usual.

The surgeon is fashioning the upper lobe bronchus, straightening it at an angle to improve resection margins. The upper lobe bronchus and left main bronchus are prepared for anastomosis. A Foro V-Lock suture, characterized by absorbable monofilament and one-way barbs, is used for continuous suturing along the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus. The suture is tightened without loosening, aided by robotic vision with eight times magnification.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing around the cartilaginous portion of the bronchial anastomosis using a V-Lock suture, ensuring tightness with each pull. Larger bites are taken in the left main bronchus compared to the left upper lobe bronchus. The upper lobe vein is visible near the suture line, and the surgeon uses robotic assistance to retract the vein for better visualization. The suture process is completed with careful alignment of the bronchial structures.

The surgeon uses a Prograsp instrument to handle the suture, noting its strong grip that can potentially damage lung tissue. After snapping the suture, a new one is taken to repeat the initial row. The V-lock suture is used for continuous suturing along the delicate membranous portion of the upper lobe bronchus, ensuring careful passage to avoid tearing.

The surgeon carefully sutures the thin membranous portion of the bronchus, using a curved needle to avoid tearing the delicate tissue. The Prograsp instrument holds the thick part of the bronchus near the secondary carina, and the surgeon advises cutting above this area to avoid thick tissue. The Prograsp's strong grip causes a suture to break, necessitating the use of a third V-lock suture, which is hand-tied to the snapped suture.

The surgeon ties the final knot between the cartilaginous and membranous sutures using a needle driver, ensuring the knot is not too tight to avoid snapping. The bronchoscopic view shows the final anastomosis, which is widely open. An underwater check confirms no bubbles from the bronchial anastomosis, indicating a successful seal. The tumor is removed through a sub-xiphoid incision, minimizing pain and rib spreading.

The surgeon checks the wide open margin of the lower lobe bronchus, ensuring a satisfactory resection. A drain is placed, and the lung is inflated. The video concludes with the final result showing five incisions and a sub-xiphoid removal.
